content.text: Continued fractions provide an alternative to decimal representations.

content.title: Simple Continued Fraction
content.content:
A **simple continued fraction** is an expression of the form:
\[
a_0 + \cfrac{1}{a_1 + \cfrac{1}{a_2 + \cfrac{1}{\ddots + \cfrac{1}{a_n}}}}
\]
where $a_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $a_1, \ldots, a_n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$.

We write this as $[a_0; a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n]$.

content.text: Every rational number has a finite continued fraction expansion.

content.title: Computing CF Expansion
content.content:
To find the continued fraction of $p/q$ ($q > 0$):

1. Let $a_0 = \lfloor p/q \rfloor$.

2. If $p = a_0 q$, stop: $p/q = [a_0]$.

3. Otherwise, set $r = p - a_0 q$ and compute the CF of $q/r$.

4. Prepend $a_0$ to get the CF of $p/q$.

content.text: This is essentially the Euclidean algorithm!

content.title: Convergent
content.content:
The $k$th **convergent** of $[a_0; a_1, \ldots, a_n]$ is 
$c_k = [a_0; a_1, \ldots, a_k]$ for $0 \leq k \leq n$.

content.text: This implies $\gcd(p_k, q_k) = 1$—each convergent is in lowest terms.
